URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electronic_literature

SUMMARY:
Electronic literature or digital literature is a genre of literature where digital capabilities such as interactivity, multimodality or algorithmic text

FULL CONTENT:
![](/static/images/icons/wikipedia.png)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)
![The Free Encyclopedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-tagline-en.svg)

## Contents

# Electronic literature

![This is a good article. Click here for more information.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/94/Symbol_support_vote.svg/20px-Symbol_support_vote.svg.png)

| Electronic literature | |
| --- | --- |
| Features | Literary works that require the capabilities of computers and networks |
| Related genres | |
| [Hypertext fiction](/wiki/Hypertext_fiction "Hypertext fiction"), [interactive fiction](/wiki/Interactive_fiction "Interactive fiction"), [digital poetry](/wiki/Digital_poetry "Digital poetry"), [generative literature](/wiki/Generative_literature "Generative literature"), [cell phone novels](/wiki/Cell_phone_novels "Cell phone novels"), [instapoetry](/wiki/Instapoetry "Instapoetry"), [cybertext](/wiki/Cybertext "Cybertext"), [netprov](/wiki/Netprov "Netprov"), [creepypasta](/wiki/Creepypasta "Creepypasta"), [fan fiction](/wiki/Fan_fiction "Fan fiction"), [web fiction](/wiki/Web_fiction "Web fiction") | |

**Electronic literature** or **digital literature** is a genre of literature where digital capabilities such as [interactivity](/wiki/Interactivity "Interactivity"), [multimodality](/wiki/Multimodality "Multimodality") or [algorithmic text generation](/wiki/Generative_literature "Generative literature") are used aesthetically.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Works of electronic literature are usually intended to be read on digital devices, such as [computers](/wiki/Computers "Computers"), [tablets](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer"), and [mobile phones](/wiki/Mobile_phones "Mobile phones"). They cannot be easily printed, or cannot be printed at all, because elements crucial to the work cannot be carried over onto a printed version.

The first literary works for computers, created in the 1950s, were computer programs that generated poems or stories, now called [generative literature](/wiki/Generative_literature "Generative literature"). In the 1960s experimental poets began to explore the new digital medium, and the first early text-based games were created. [Interactive fiction](/wiki/Interactive_fiction "Interactive fiction") became a popular genre in the late 1970s and 1980s, with a thriving online community in the 2000s. In the 1980s and 1990s [hypertext fiction](/wiki/Hypertext_fiction "Hypertext fiction") begun to be published, first on [floppy disks](/wiki/Floppy_disk "Floppy disk") and later on the [web](/wiki/World_Wide_Web "World Wide Web"). Hypertext fictions are stories where the reader moves from page to page by selecting links. In the 2000s [digital poetry](/wiki/Digital_poetry "Digital poetry") became popular, often including animated text, images and interactivity. In the 2010s and 2020s, electronic literature uses [social media platforms](/wiki/Social_media_platforms "Social media platforms"), with new genres like [Instapoetry](/wiki/Instapoetry "Instapoetry") or [Twitterature](/wiki/Twitterature "Twitterature") as well as literary practices like [netprov](/wiki/Netprov "Netprov"). Although web-based genres like [creepypasta](/wiki/Creepypasta "Creepypasta") and [fan fiction](/wiki/Fan_fiction "Fan fiction") are not always thought of as electronic literature (because they usually manifest as linear texts that could be printed out and read on paper) other scholars argue that these are born digital genres that depend on online communities and thus should be included in the field.

There is an extensive body of scholarship on electronic literature. In 1999 the [Electronic Literature Organization](/wiki/Electronic_Literature_Organization "Electronic Literature Organization") was established, which through annual conferences and other events supports both the publishing and study of electronic literature. One focus of academic study has been the preservation and archiving of works of electronic literature. This is challenging because works become impossible to access or read when the software or hardware they are designed for becomes obsolete. In addition, works of electronic literature are not part of the established publishing industry and so do not have ISBN numbers and are not findable in library catalogues. This has led to the establishment of a number of archives and documentation projects.

## Definitions

The literary critic and professor [N. Katherine Hayles](/wiki/N._Katherine_Hayles "N. Katherine Hayles") defines electronic literature as "'digital born' (..) and (usually) meant to be read on a computer",[[2]](#cite_note-2) clarifying that this does not include e-books and digitised print literature.

A definition offered by the [Electronic Literature Organization](/wiki/Electronic_Literature_Organization "Electronic Literature Organization") (ELO) states that electronic literature "refers to works with an important literary aspect that takes advantage of the capabilities and contexts provided by the stand-alone or networked computer".[[3]](#cite_note-:11-3) This can include [hypertext fiction](/wiki/Hypertext_fiction "Hypertext fiction"), animated poetry (often called kinetic poetry) and other forms of [digital poetry](/wiki/Digital_poetry "Digital poetry"), literary chatbots, [computer-generated narratives or poetry](/wiki/Generative_literature "Generative literature"), art installations with significant literary aspects, [interactive fiction](/wiki/Interactive_fiction "Interactive fiction") and literary uses of social media.[[3]](#cite_note-:11-3)

For example, a [hypertext fiction](/wiki/Hypertext_fiction "Hypertext fiction") is a story where the reader chooses a path through the story by clicking on links that connect fragments of text, often called [lexias](/wiki/Lexia_(hypertext) "Lexia (hypertext)").[[4]](#cite_note-4) In [digital poetry](/wiki/Digital_poetry "Digital poetry") the words in the poem may move across the screen or may involve game-like interactivity.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In [generative literature](/wiki/Generative_literature "Generative literature") a single work can generate many different poems or stories. Until the early 2000s electronic literature works tended to be published on [floppy disk](/wiki/Floppy_disk "Floppy disk"), [CD-ROM](/wiki/CD-ROM "CD-ROM"), in online literary journals or on dedicated websites. However, since around 2010 literary genres on [social media platforms](/wiki/Social_media "Social media") - such as [Instapoetry](/wiki/Instapoetry "Instapoetry"), [Twitterature](/wiki/Twitterature "Twitterature") or [netprov](/wiki/Netprov "Netprov") - have come to be seen as electronic literature. The literary critic Leonardo Flores called these third generation electronic literature, following the first generation of pre-web works and the second generation of web-based works.[[6]](#cite_note-:12-6) Flores uses an inclusive definition of electronic literature, which can include social media posts with literary qualities even if the authors do not themselves think of it as literature.[[6]](#cite_note-:12-6) Fan fiction and creepypasta have also been analysed as electronic literature.[[7]](#cite_note-Rettberg_2019_3-7): 109

The definition of electronic literature is controversial within the field, with strict definitions being criticised for excluding valuable works, and looser definitions being so murky as to be useless.[[7]](#cite_note-Rettberg_2019_3-7) A work of electronic literature can be defined as "a construction whose literary aesthetics emerge from computation", "work that could only exist in the space for which it was developed/written/coded—the digital space".[[8]](#cite_note-8) In his book *Electronic Literature*, the author and scholar [Scott Rettberg](/wiki/Scott_Rettberg "Scott Rettberg") argues that an advantage of a wide definition is its flexibility, which allows it to include new genres as new platforms and modes of literature emerge.[[7]](#cite_note-Rettberg_2019_3-7) Screenwriter and author Carolyn Handler Miller characterizes works of electronic literature as nonlinear and non chronological where the user experiences and co-creates the story, and where contradictory events and different outcomes are possible.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## History

### Precursors

Scholars have discussed a range of pre-digital precursors to electronic literature, from the ancient Chinese book the *[I Ching](/wiki/I_Ching "I Ching")*,[[10]](#cite_note-Aarseth1997-10): 9 to inventor [John Clark](/wiki/John_Clark_(inventor) "John Clark (inventor)")'s mechanical [Latin Verse Machine](/wiki/Latin_Verse_Machine "Latin Verse Machine") (1830-1843)[[11]](#cite_note-11) to the [Dadaist](/wiki/Dadaist "Dadaist") movement's [cut-up technique](/wiki/Cut-up_technique "Cut-up technique").[[7]](#cite_note-Rettberg_2019_3-7) Print novels that were designed to be read non-linearly, such as [Julio Cortázar](/wiki/Julio_Cort%C3%A1zar "Julio Cortázar")'s [*Hopscotch*](/wiki/Hopscotch_(Cort%C3%A1zar_novel) "Hopscotch (Cortázar novel)") (1963) and [Vladimir Nabokov](/wiki/Vladimir_Nabokov "Vladimir Nabokov")'s *[Pale Fire](/wiki/Pale_Fire "Pale Fire")* (1962), are cited as "print antecedents" of electronic literature.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### 1950s

The 1952 [love letter generator](/wiki/Strachey_love_letter_algorithm "Strachey love letter algorithm") that the British computer scientist [Christopher Strachey](/wiki/Christopher_Strachey "Christopher Strachey") wrote for the [Manchester Mark 1](/wiki/Manchester_Mark_1 "Manchester Mark 1") computer is probably the first example of literature that requires a computer to be generated or read.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) The work generates short love letters, and is an example of combinatory poetry, also called [generative poetry](/wiki/Generative_literature "Generative literature").[[16]](#cite_note-16) The original code has been lost, but digital poet and scholar [Nick Montfort](/wiki/Nick_Montfort "Nick Montfort") has reimplemented it based on remaining documentation of its output, and this version can be viewed in a web browser.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In 1959 the German computer scientist [Theo Lutz](/w/index.php?title=Theo_Lutz&action=edit&redlink=1 "Theo Lutz (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Theo_Lutz "de:Theo Lutz")] wrote *Stochastic Texts*, which "for many years was considered the first digital literary text."[[18]](#cite_note-Di_Rosario_2021-18): 11 *Stochastic Texts* was a program written for a [Z22](/wiki/Z22_(computer) "Z22 (computer)") computer that "produced random short sentences based on a corpus of chapter titles and subjects from [Franz Kafka](/wiki/Franz_Kafka "Franz Kafka")'s novel *[The Castle](/wiki/The_Castle_(novel) "The Castle (novel)")*.[[7]](#cite_note-Rettberg_2019_3-7) Lutz's work has been discussed both as a very early work of electronic literature[[19]](#cite_note-:2-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) and as an important precursor to current AI-generated literature.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The German philosopher and media scholar [Hannes Bajohr](/w/index.php?title=Hannes_Bajohr&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hannes Bajohr (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hannes_Bajohr "de:Hannes Bajohr")] writes that *Stochastic Texts* is an example of the "sequential paradigm" in [generative literature](/wiki/Generative_literature "Generative literature"), in opposition to newer examples of a "connectionist paradigm": "Instead of hoping to recreate intuition, genius, or expression, the logic of the machine itself – that is, the logic of deterministically executed rule steps – becomes aesthetically normative in *Stochastische Texte*."[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### 1960s

The 1960s were a time of literary experimentation, and there were strong connections between the art and technology scenes and [concrete poetry](/wiki/Concrete_poetry "Concrete poetry").[[24]](#cite_note-24) The Italian poet and artist [Nanni Balestrini](/wiki/Nanni_Balestrini "Nanni Balestrini")'s poem *Tape Mark I* was composed in 1961 on an [IBM 7070](/wiki/IBM_7070 "IBM 7070"), and output from the poetry generator was published in a special issue of a journal edited by the novelist and scholar [Umberto Eco](/wiki/Umberto_Eco "Umberto Eco") and artist [Bruno Munari](/wiki/Bruno_Munari "Bruno Munari"), thus standing as the first Italian work of electronic literature.[[25]](#cite_note-25) *Auto-Beatnik* (1961) was a program by R. M. Worthy and colleagues at the computer manufacturing company [Librascope](/wiki/Librascope "Librascope").[[26]](#cite_note-26) *Auto-Beatnik* generated poems on an [LGP-30](/wiki/LGP-30 "LGP-30") computer to mimic the style of [Beat poetry](/wiki/Beat_Generation "Beat Generation").[[7]](#cite_note-Rettberg_2019_3-7)

Games designers [Mabel Addis](/wiki/Mabel_Addis "Mabel Addis") and William McKay's text-based narrative game [The Sumerian Game](/wiki/The_Sumerian_Game "The Sumerian Game") (1964–66) was probably the first narrative computer game, although it was not widely distributed.[[27]](#cite_note-:3-27) The computer scientist [Joseph Weizenbaum](/wiki/Joseph_Weizenbaum "Joseph Weizenbaum") programmed the chatbot [ELIZA](/wiki/ELIZA "ELIZA") in 1966, establishing a new genre of conversational literary artefacts or bots.[[28]](#cite_note-28) This was the decade when the sociologist and philosopher [Ted Nelson](/wiki/Ted_Nelson "Ted Nelson") coined the terms [hypertext](/wiki/Hypertext "Hypertext") and [hypermedia](/wiki/Hypermedia "Hypermedia").[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### 1970s

Writers and artists continued to experiment with combining art, technology and literature. An example is the installation *[Blikk](/wiki/Blikk_(artwork) "Blikk (artwork)")* (1970) by a Norwegian trio: artist [Irma Salo Jæger](/w/index.php?title=Irma_Salo_J%C3%A6ger&action=edit&redlink=1 "Irma Salo Jæger (page does not exist)") [[no](https://no.wikipedia.org/wiki/Irma_Salo_J%C3%A6ger "no:Irma Salo Jæger")], composer [Sigurd Berge](/wiki/Sigurd_Berge "Sigurd Berge") and poet [Jan Erik Vold](/wiki/Jan_Erik_Vold "Jan Erik Vold").[[30]](#cite_note-30) Vold's readings of his poems were mixed as sound montages by Berge and combined with Jæger's kinetic sculptures in an exhibition at the [Henie Onstad Art Center](/wiki/Henie_Onstad_Art_Center "Henie Onstad Art Center"). The work was recreated in 2022 by the composer and curator [Jøran Rudi](/w/index.php?title=J%C3%B8ran_Rudi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jøran Rudi (page does not exist)") [[no](https://no.wikipedia.org/wiki/J%C3%B8ran_Rudi "no:Jøran Rudi")] and is now part of the permanent collection of the [Norwegian National Museum](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Art,_Architecture_and_Design "National Museum of Art, Architecture and Design").[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Another important development in the 1970s was the popularity of text adventure games, now more commonly known as interactive fiction. In 1975–76, [Will Crowther](/wiki/William_Crowther_(programmer) "William Crowther (programmer)") programmed a text game named *[Colossal Cave Adventure](/wiki/Colossal_Cave_Adventure "Colossal Cave Adventure")* (also known as *Adventure* or *ADVENT*). It possessed a story that had the reader make choices on which way to go. These choices could lead the reader to the end, or to their untimely death. It is often regarded as the first work of interactive fiction,[[7]](#cite_note-Rettberg_2019_3-7) although others have argued that the simulated microworld [SHRDLU](/wiki/SHRDLU "SHRDLU")[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) or [Mabel Addis](/wiki/Mabel_Addis "Mabel Addis")'s *[The Sumerian Game](/wiki/The_Sumerian_Game "The Sumerian Game")*[[27]](#cite_note-:3-27) were earlier and should be considered interactive fiction. Historians agree that *Colossal Cave Adventure* made a "significant cultural impact" in the 1970s.[[34]](#cite_note-34) It has been called a "classic"[[35]](#cite_note-35) "so foundational it started a genre",[[36]](#cite_note-36) "the [*Gilgamesh*](/wiki/Epic_of_Gilgamesh "Epic of Gilgamesh") of video games",[[37]](#cite_note-:4-37) and is credited with having "informed and inspired generations of players."[[37]](#cite_note-:4-37) *Colossal Cave Adventure* was played on [mainframe computers](/wiki/Mainframe_computer "Mainframe computer"), and spread rapidly through the [ARPANET](/wiki/ARPANET "ARPANET"). *Colossal Cave* inspired many other games, including the text adventure game *[Zork](/wiki/Zork "Zork")* (1977) which was regarded as one of the best known.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### 1980s

With the advent of [personal computers](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer"), interactive fiction became a commercially successful genre, driven by companies like [Infocom](/wiki/Infocom "Infocom"). Companies hired authors and programmers to write text adventure games, as [Veronika Megler](/wiki/Veronika_Megler "Veronika Megler"), who wrote [*The Hobbit*](/wiki/The_Hobbit_(1982_video_game) "The Hobbit (1982 video game)") video game in 1982, described in an interview with *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

For [hypertext fiction](/wiki/Hypertext_fiction "Hypertext fiction") and [digital poetry](/wiki/Digital_poetry "Digital poetry"), the eighties were a time of experimentation in separate communities that were not necessarily aware of each other. In Canada, the poet [Bp Nichol](/wiki/Bp_Nichol "Bp Nichol") published *First Screening: Computer Poems*, written in [BASIC](/wiki/BASIC "BASIC"), in 1984.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The Californian writer [Judy Malloy](/wiki/Judy_Malloy "Judy Malloy") published *Uncle Roger* on the online community [The WELL](/wiki/The_WELL "The WELL") in 1986/87.[[41]](#cite_note-:1-41) On the East Coast, hypertext fiction was being explored by academics and writers who met at the [ACM Hypertext conference](/wiki/ACM_Conference_on_Hypertext_and_Social_Media "ACM Conference on Hypertext and Social Media"), which held its inaugural meeting in 1987.[[42]](#cite_note-42) [Michael Joyce](/wiki/Michael_Joyce_(writer) "Michael Joyce (writer)")'s *[afternoon, a story](/wiki/Afternoon,_a_story "Afternoon, a story")*, one of the most cited works of hypertext fiction, was demonstrated at the 1987 conference, and [Mark Bernstein](/wiki/Mark_Bernstein_(publisher) "Mark Bernstein (publisher)") published this work at [Eastgate Systems](/wiki/Eastgate_Systems "Eastgate Systems").[[41]](#cite_note-:1-41) The hypertext author [Stuart Moulthrop](/wiki/Stuart_Moulthrop "Stuart Moulthrop") described discovering writer and visual artist [Judy Malloy](/wiki/Judy_Malloy "Judy Malloy")'s work at this time, not having realised that there were other people writing literature for computers: "I can remember coming away from that moment thinking that, you know, there might be a real hope for what we were trying to do because other people were doing it".[[41]](#cite_note-:1-41)

In France at this time, *literature numérique* (digital literature) was connected to the [Oulipo](/wiki/Oulipo "Oulipo") literary movement, and poetry was more central to the French writers than the narrative genres like hypertext fiction that were popular in the United States.[[43]](#cite_note-:13-43) Generative poetry could be seen as a particularly European genre at the time.[[43]](#cite_note-:13-43) In 1981 the Portuguese author [Pedro Barbosa](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_Barbosa_(writer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pedro Barbosa (writer) (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pedro_Barbosa_(escritor) "pt:Pedro Barbosa (escritor)")] published the combinartory work *THE ALAMO*, and explicitly made the claim that computationally generated works could be literary.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

Not only writers, but also digital artists created works with strong literary components that have had an influence on the field of electronic literature. An example is the Australian artist [Jeffrey Shaw](/wiki/Jeffrey_Shaw "Jeffrey Shaw") and Dirk Groeneveld's *The Legible City*, which was first exhibited at [ZKM Center for Art and Media Karlsruhe](/wiki/ZKM_Center_for_Art_and_Media_Karlsruhe "ZKM Center for Art and Media Karlsruhe") in 1988.[[45]](#cite_note-45) *The Legible City* is an art installation where the visitor rides a stationary bicycle through a simulated city displayed as computer-generated text. Buildings and streets are shown as 3D shapes consisting of letters and words, allowing the reader to "read" the city as they pedal through it.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### 1990s

![Photo of](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Stuart_Moulthrop%27s_hypertext_fiction_Victory_Garden_exhibited_at_HT23.jpg/250px-Stuart_Moulthrop%27s_hypertext_fiction_Victory_Garden_exhibited_at_HT23.jpg)

The "Storyspace school" characterised the early 1990s in the United States,[[41]](#cite_note-:1-41) consisting of works created using [Storyspace](/wiki/Storyspace "Storyspace"), hypertext authoring software developed by the literary scholar [Jay David Bolter](/wiki/Jay_David_Bolter "Jay David Bolter") and the author [Michael Joyce](/wiki/Michael_Joyce_(writer) "Michael Joyce (writer)") in the 1980s.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Bolter and Joyce sold the Storyspace software in 1990 to [Eastgate Systems](/wiki/Eastgate_Systems "Eastgate Systems"), a small software company that became a publishing house and the main distributor of hypertext fiction in the 1990s, particularly in the early 1990s before it was possible to publish works on the [web](/wiki/World_Wide_Web "World Wide Web").[[18]](#cite_note-Di_Rosario_2021-18): 17–18 Eastgate has maintained and updated the code in Storyspace up to the present.[[48]](#cite_note-Barnet-48) Storyspace and similar programs use [hypertext](/wiki/Hypertext "Hypertext") to create links within text. Literature using hypertext is frequently referred to as [hypertext fiction](/wiki/Hypertext_fiction "Hypertext fiction"). Originally, these stories were often disseminated on [discs](/wiki/Floppy_disk "Floppy disk") and later on [CD-ROM](/wiki/CD-ROM "CD-ROM").[[49]](#cite_note-Aarseth_1997-49) Hypertext fiction is still being created today using not only Storyspace, but other programs such as [Twine](/wiki/Twine_(software) "Twine (software)").[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[41]](#cite_note-:1-41)

This period is often termed the first generation hypertext era, as [N. Katherine Hayles](/wiki/N._Katherine_Hayles "N. Katherine Hayles") notes that these works used lexia or separate screens in a similar manner to books and pages.[[51]](#cite_note-:14-51) In a 1993 article for the [New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times") Book Review, "Hyperfiction: Novels for the Computer", the novelist and professor [Robert Coover](/wiki/Robert_Coover "Robert Coover") noted the new possibilities for exploring these various storyworlds: "[I]t is a strange place, hyperspace, much more like inner space than outer, a space not of coordinates but of the volumeless imagination".[[52]](#cite_note-:9-52) Key works from this period include [Stuart Moulthrop](/wiki/Stuart_Moulthrop "Stuart Moulthrop")'s [*Victory Garden*](/wiki/Victory_Garden_(novel) "Victory Garden (novel)"), [Shelley Jackson](/wiki/Shelley_Jackson "Shelley Jackson")'s [*Patchwork Girl*](/wiki/Patchwork_Girl_(hypertext) "Patchwork Girl (hypertext)") (1995) and [Deena Larsen](/wiki/Deena_Larsen "Deena Larsen")'s work.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

Towards the middle of the decade, authors began writing on the web. [Stuart Moulthrop](/wiki/Stuart_Moulthrop "Stuart Moulthrop")'s *Hegirascope* and [Mark Amerika](/wiki/Mark_Amerika "Mark Amerika")'s *Hypertextual Consciousness* were both published in 1995. Other early web-based hypertext fictions include [Olia Lialina](/wiki/Olia_Lialina "Olia Lialina")'s *[My Boyfriend Came Back from the War](/wiki/My_Boyfriend_Came_Back_from_the_War "My Boyfriend Came Back from the War")*, [Adrienne Eisen](/wiki/Adrienne_Eisen "Adrienne Eisen")'s *[Six Sex Scenes](/wiki/Six_Sex_Scenes "Six Sex Scenes")* and Robert Arellano's *Sunshine '69*, all published in 1996.[[54]](#cite_note-54) [Mark Amerika](/wiki/Mark_Amerika "Mark Amerika") released *GRAMMATRON* on June 26, 1997, the same day the work was featured in the New York Times. [[55]](#cite_note-55) [Scott Rettberg](/wiki/Scott_Rettberg "Scott Rettberg"), William Gillespie, Dirk Stratton, and Frank Marquadt's sprawling hypertext novel *[The Unknown](/wiki/The_Unknown_(hypertext_novel) "The Unknown (hypertext novel)")* won the trAce/Alt-X Hypertext Competition in 1998.[[56]](#cite_note-56) It was featured in the Electronic Literature Collection Vol. 2,[[57]](#cite_note-57) and has been analysed by a number of scholars.[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)

The [Electronic Literature Organization](/wiki/Electronic_Literature_Organization "Electronic Literature Organization") (the ELO) was founded in 1999 by hypertext author [Scott Rettberg](/wiki/Scott_Rettberg "Scott Rettberg"), the author and teacher of creative writing [Robert Coover](/wiki/Robert_Coover "Robert Coover") and internet investor Jeff Ballowe, with the mission "to facilitate and promote the writing, publishing, and reading of literature in electronic media".[[62]](#cite_note-62) The ELO is still active today, with annual conferences, online discussions and publications.[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64)

The late 1990s also saw the first online [literary journals](/wiki/Literary_magazine "Literary magazine"), also known as [webzines](/wiki/Webzines "Webzines") or e-zines. Online journals that published electronic literature included *Beehive*, founded by Talan Mammott in 1998, *The Iowa Review Web*, founded by Thom Swiss in 1999, *Riding the Meridian*, founded by Jennifer Ley in 1999, *Cauldron.net*, founded by Claire Dinsmore in 1999, *[Drunken Boat](/wiki/Drunken_Boat_(literary_journal) "Drunken Boat (literary journal)")*, founded by [Ravi Shankar](/wiki/Ravi_Shankar_(poet) "Ravi Shankar (poet)") and Michael Mills in 2000, and *poemsthatgo.com*, founded by Megan Sapnar and Ingrid Ankerson in 2000.[[65]](#cite_note-65) The *[electronic book review](/wiki/Electronic_Book_Review "Electronic Book Review")* was an online journal founded by [Joseph Tabbi](/wiki/Joseph_Tabbi "Joseph Tabbi") that published criticism and essays often discussing electronic literature.

### 2000s

![Network visualisation showing titles of works clustered in four groups, each corresponding to a genre: Interactive fiction, web hypertexts, hypertext fictions (mostly on disk) and generative works.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Genres_of_electronic_literature_2002-2008_shown_in_a_network_visualisation.jpg/330px-Genres_of_electronic_literature_2002-2008_shown_in_a_network_visualisation.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Genres_of_electronic_literature_2009-2013_shown_in_a_network_visualisation.jpg/330px-Genres_of_electronic_literature_2009-2013_shown_in_a_network_visualisation.jpg)

In Japan, [cell phone novels](/wiki/Cell_phone_novel "Cell phone novel") became popular from the early 2000s.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Similar genres emerged in other countries where [text messaging](/wiki/Text_messaging "Text messaging") was well-established, including India[[68]](#cite_note-:10-68) and Europe.[[69]](#cite_note-69) The first work of Indian electronic literature is probably the 2004 SMS novel *Cloak Room*,[[70]](#cite_note-70) whose author used the pseudonym RoGue. *Cloak Room* invited readers to engage with the story by answering texts or leaving comments on the blog that was used in tandem with the text messages.[[68]](#cite_note-:10-68)[[71]](#cite_note-71)

In North America the [web](/wiki/World_Wide_Web "World Wide Web") was becoming the main platform for electronic literature. The Canadian author [Caitlin Fisher](/wiki/Caitlin_Fisher "Caitlin Fisher")'s *[These Waves of Girls](/wiki/These_Waves_of_Girls "These Waves of Girls")* (2001) was a hypermedia novella telling stories of girlhood, using images and sounds as well as links and text.[[72]](#cite_note-72) The American author Talan Memmott's *[Lexia to Perplexia](/wiki/Lexia_to_Perplexia "Lexia to Perplexia")* (2000) offered complex visual and textual layers that sometimes confuse and occlude themselves,[[73]](#cite_note-73) and is described by the literary critic Lisa Swanstrom as a "beautifully intricate piece of electronic literature".[[74]](#cite_note-74) [Kate Pullinger](/wiki/Kate_Pullinger "Kate Pullinger")'s *[Inanimate Alice](/wiki/Inanimate_Alice "Inanimate Alice")* is an example of a work that began as a web novel and then saw versions across several media, including a screenplay and a [VR](/wiki/Virtual_reality "Virtual reality") experience.[[75]](#cite_note-75) Early works were concerned with erasure and experiemented with technology. Works like *[The Impermanence Agent](/wiki/The_Impermanence_Agent "The Impermanence Agent")*, by author and scholar [Noah Wardrip-Fruin](/wiki/Noah_Wardrip-Fruin "Noah Wardrip-Fruin") and collaborators, explored the web's ability to customize a story for the reader.[[76]](#cite_note-76) Works like *database* by [Adriana de Souza e Silva](/wiki/Adriana_de_Souza_e_Silva "Adriana de Souza e Silva") used a printer, video camera, and a database to erase stories "in real time".[[77]](#cite_note-:02-77)

An analysis of 44 PhD dissertations about electronic literature published between 2002 and 2013[[41]](#cite_note-:1-41) found a clear shift in the genres referenced by the authors of the dissertations during this period. Between 2002 and 2008, the referenced works clustered in four distinct genre groups: [interactive fiction](/wiki/Interactive_fiction "Interactive fiction"), [generative literature](/wiki/Generative_literature "Generative literature"), classic [hypertext fiction](/wiki/Hypertext_fiction "Hypertext fiction") (mostly publish

enriched_points.expanded_content: Digital literature analysis involves examining literary works created or published digitally, emphasizing interactivity, multimedia, and technology, differentiating from traditional print literature.
This analysis is crucial in literary studies as it broadens understanding of narrative forms and reader engagement, reflecting contemporary reading habits shaped by digital technology.
The rise of digital tools has revolutionized literature creation, enabling authors to craft interactive and multimedia-rich works, leading to new genres and storytelling methods.

Digital literature analysis intersects various fields including literature, computer science, and media studies, fostering collaboration and innovation in understanding narrative structures and technologies.
Digital tools transform research methodologies, allowing for computational analysis of texts, enhancing accessibility and enabling new frameworks for literary criticism and engagement.
